lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of plastic bathtubs, shower stalls, sinks, lavatories, toilet fixtures, and related plastic plumbing fixtures supplied repeatedly to distributors, builders, remodelers, hospitality customers, and OEM or private-label accounts.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, installers without fixture manufacturing, metal or vitreous-china fixture makers, distributors without manufacturing, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat B2B product sales through distributor, wholesale, builder, OEM, and project channels, generally priced per unit or product line with periodic negotiated adjustments and meaningful freight and resin exposure.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation and wage mix for broader NAICS 326100 Plastics Product Manufacturing, not task-level AI exposure for plumbing fixtures. I map administrative, sales, engineering-support, scheduling, quality-documentation, maintenance-triage, and limited machine-monitoring work into exposed wages; broader-product composition and unknown current automation can bias the estimate in either direction.
a.rationale: Production occupations are 58.94 percent of broader plastics-product employment, which limits direct generative-AI substitution. The reachable opportunity is concentrated in quoting, demand and production planning, product documentation, customer service, quality analytics, inspection support, and maintenance rather than hands-on molding and finishing.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census from 8-12 independent fixture plants, with current automation flags and time studies for quoting, CAD support, scheduling, QA, inspection, maintenance, and customer administration.
caveats: Four-digit occupational data are not specific to 326191.
Exposure is inferred from occupational structure rather than directly observed tasks.

rho.bridge: BLS evidence covers metal and plastic machine workers across industries and describes adoption of CNC, robotics, and software, not five-year AI implementation in small plumbing-fixture plants. Translating this into implementation likely overstates smaller firms with old molds, varied finishes, manual trim, and scarce integration capacity.
rho.rationale: Office and planning workflows can change quickly, while molded-fixture production must preserve finish quality, dimensional consistency, safety, and line uptime. Vision, maintenance, and scheduling deployments are feasible but integration and capex leave material exposure unimplemented.
rho.change_evidence: Plant automation roadmaps, MES and machine-vision penetration, integrator proposals, implementation paybacks, and post-deployment labor-hour evidence from fixture molders.
caveats: The machine-worker source includes processes unlike thermoforming, injection molding, and composite fixture finishing.
Product variety and manual finishing materially affect implementability.

e.rationale: The industry is a coherent external product-supply activity with repeat channels, but the eligible share is reduced by captive or brand-controlled plants, project-heavy revenue, low-quality assets, customer concentration, environmental liabilities, and firms outside normalized LMM control economics.
e.change_evidence: D&B or Data Axle ownership mapping for every establishment, paired with channel mix, captive status, recurring revenue, customer concentration, EBITDA normalization, and broker-quality asset information.
caveats: NAICS scope confirms activity but not ownership or transferability.
The frozen firm count is a margin-based estimate, not observed eligible firms.

s5.bridge: Gallup observes intentions of US employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying transfers among eligible plastics plumbing-fixture manufacturers. I discount intentions for non-completion, internal family or employee transfers, strategic absorption, and liquidation; the broad survey may overstate externally purchasable controls.
s5.rationale: General succession pressure supports availability, but this small industrial niche has no observed denominator-matched five-year transaction rate. Tooling, brands, channel relationships, and plant liabilities can cause intended exits to fail or become asset sales.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year closed-deal records from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, plastics M&A advisers, and business brokers matched against a verified eligible-firm universe and supplemented with owner-age data.
caveats: Intentions are not transactions and the source is cross-industry.
No six-digit succession or completed-control dataset was found.

q.rationale: Proprietary molds, design, finish quality, certification, and reliable supply support retention, especially during product cycles. Distributors, builders, large retailers, and OEM customers exert price pressure and periodically reset pricing, so visible cost savings are partly competed or negotiated away.
q.change_evidence: Customer agreements, price lists and rebate schedules, renewal and line-review histories, resin and freight pass-through clauses, and SKU-level margins before and after automation.
caveats: No public contract set quantifies benefit pass-through.
Private-label commodity fixtures likely retain less than differentiated branded or engineered products.

d5.rationale: Replacement, remodeling, new construction, and water-efficiency upgrades create continuing fixture demand. Housing and commercial construction cycles, imports, alternative materials, and product durability limit growth, while water-efficiency standards and retrofit programs support replacement and product refresh.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price domestic shipment units by fixture class, distributor sell-through, housing-completion and remodeling forecasts, WaterSense-certified product replacement data, and major customer purchasing plans.
caveats: WaterSense documents product efficiency and replacement logic, not a five-year industry volume forecast.
Demand varies by new-build, remodel, institutional, and manufactured-housing exposure.

o.rationale: The service basket is physical molded-product supply requiring tooling, materials control, testing, quality accountability, and logistics. AI software can support those functions but cannot replace the accountable manufacturer; insourcing by major brands and substitution to other fixture materials are the principal displacement paths.
o.change_evidence: Customer insourcing announcements, contract-manufacturing renewal rates, fixture-material substitution studies, and evidence of software-only or unattended autonomous supply models.
caveats: Operator requirement is inferred from the physical and performance-tested product.
Material substitution is mainly captured in d5 to avoid double counting.

narrative.executive_view: Plastics plumbing fixtures combine repeat physical-product channels with useful but bounded digital and automation opportunities. Improvement is most plausible in planning, quoting, quality, inspection, maintenance, and administration; tooling, molding, finishing, and customer acceptance remain operator-intensive.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can improve demand planning, production scheduling, CAD and document retrieval, quoting, quality analytics, vision-assisted inspection, maintenance triage, and customer support. Manual trim, finishing, handling, setup, and process troubleshooting are harder to substitute within five years.
narrative.value_capture: Mold ownership, finish quality, design differentiation, certification, and delivery reliability can protect some savings. Distributor, retailer, builder, and OEM bargaining power plus line reviews and competitive imports should erode a meaningful portion over time.
narrative.firm_availability: The activity is coherent but the true pool of independent, recurring-supply, LMM firms is uncertain. Succession pressure is plausible; verified ownership, EBITDA, asset quality, channels, and closed transfer data are absent.
narrative.demand_durability: Construction and remodeling cycles cause volatility, while replacement and efficiency-led product refresh support recurring demand. Physical fixtures still require an accountable manufacturer even as software and factory automation advance.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key risks include housing cyclicality, import competition, resin and freight volatility, large-customer concentration, obsolete tooling, warranty and product-liability exposure, and high manual finishing content. The evidence is weakest on six-digit occupation mix, firm eligibility, transfer incidence, and contract-level capture.
narrative.principal_driver: Recurring replacement and construction channels plus improvable planning, quality, and production-support workflows.
narrative.principal_weakness: Cyclical demand and powerful channels can absorb operational gains while plant integration remains capital-intensive.

sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 326100 Plastics Product Manufacturing
sources.supports: States that the estimates cover NAICS 326100 employers and reports 357,010 production workers, 58.94 percent of industry employment, grounding the occupation-mix proxy for AI exposure.

sources.title: Metal and Plastic Machine Workers, Occupational Outlook Handbook
sources.supports: Reports a 7 percent projected employment decline for 2024-2034 and describes ongoing expansion of CNC, robots, and other labor-saving machinery alongside continuing operator skill requirements.

sources.title: Plastics Plumbing Fixture Manufacturing: 2002 Economic Census
sources.supports: Identifies NAICS 326191 as Plastics Plumbing Fixture Manufacturing and provides the industry-series scope used to anchor the physical manufacturing lens.

sources.title: About WaterSense
sources.supports: Explains that WaterSense-labeled plumbing products meet EPA efficiency and performance criteria and provides examples of fixture replacement savings, supporting efficiency-led product replacement and testing requirements.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports a fall 2024 employer-business-owner survey about five-year plans and that 74 percent of those planning around retirement intend to sell or transfer ownership, used only as a cross-industry transfer proxy.
